{{Short description|Set of equations describing electromagnetism and light}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Maxwell's equations
| image = James Clerk Maxwell.png
| caption = [[James Clerk Maxwell]], who unified electricity, magnetism, and optics
| formula = Four partial differential equations (see below)
| discovered_by = [[James Clerk Maxwell]] (1861–1862)
}}

'''Maxwell's equations''' are a set of four [[partial differential equation]]s that form the foundation of [[classical electromagnetism]], [[optics|classical optics]], and [[electrical circuit|electric circuits]]. They describe how [[electric charge|electric]] and [[magnetic field]]s are generated by charges, currents, and changes of each other.

The equations were published by [[James Clerk Maxwell]] in 1861–1862. One major result of these equations is the prediction of [[electromagnetic wave]]s traveling at the [[speed of light]], leading to the conclusion that light is an electromagnetic phenomenon.

== The equations ==

=== In vacuum (microscopic form) ===

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Name !! Differential form !! Integral form
|-
| [[Gauss's law]]
| <math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}</math>
| <math>\oiint_{\partial V} \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}</math>
|-
| [[Gauss's law for magnetism]]
| <math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0</math>
| <math>\oiint_{\partial V} \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = 0</math>
|-
| [[Faraday's law of induction]]
| <math>\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}</math>
| <math>\oint_{\partial S} \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\iint_S \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A}</math>
|-
| [[Ampère's circuital law|Ampère–Maxwell law]]
| <math>\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0\mathbf{J} + \mu_0\varepsilon_0\frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}</math>
| <math>\oint_{\partial S} \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}} + \mu_0\varepsilon_0\frac{d}{dt}\iint_S \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A}</math>
|}

=== In matter (macroscopic form) ===
In materials, the equations involve auxiliary fields <math>\mathbf{D}</math> and <math>\mathbf{H}</math>:
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_f</math>
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0</math>
:<math>\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}</math>
:<math>\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J}_f + \frac{\partial \mathbf{D}}{\partial t}</math>

with constitutive relations <math>\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon\mathbf{E}</math> and <math>\mathbf{B} = \mu\mathbf{H}</math>.

== Physical meaning ==

=== Gauss's law ===
Electric charges create electric fields that diverge from positive charges and converge on negative charges. The total [[electric flux]] through a closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by <math>\varepsilon_0</math>.

=== Gauss's law for magnetism ===
There are no [[magnetic monopole]]s; magnetic field lines always form closed loops. The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero.

=== Faraday's law ===
A changing magnetic field creates (induces) a circulating electric field. This is the principle behind [[electrical generator]]s and [[transformer]]s.

=== Ampère–Maxwell law ===
Electric currents and changing electric fields create magnetic fields. Maxwell's key addition was the [[displacement current]] term <math>\mu_0\varepsilon_0\frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}</math>, which completes the symmetry and predicts electromagnetic waves.

== Electromagnetic waves ==
In a vacuum (<math>\rho = 0, \mathbf{J} = 0</math>), the equations decouple into wave equations:
:<math>\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{E}}{\partial t^2}, \quad \nabla^2 \mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{B}}{\partial t^2}</math>
where <math>c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}}</math>.

== Gauge invariance ==
Maxwell's equations possess [[gauge invariance]]. The fields can be expressed in terms of [[electromagnetic potential|potentials]]:
:<math>\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}, \quad \mathbf{E} = -\nabla\phi - \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electromagnetism]]
* [[Lorentz force]]
* [[Electromagnetic wave]]
* [[Quantum electrodynamics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Maxwell's equations| ]]
[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Partial differential equations]]
